text: Total laparoscopic first IMV approach for splenic flexural tumor. Forty-nine-year-old female presents with anemia and blood stall. Colonoscopy shows a splenic flexural tumor. Biopsy reported as mucinous adenocarcinoma. There is no evidence of metastasis. The patient is placed in supreme position with right arm tucked.

The surgeon inserts a 10mm trocar above the umbilicus for a 30-degree camera, along with a 10mm port in the right lower quadrant, a 5mm trocar in the right upper quadrant, and a 5mm trocar in the left quadrant for assistance. The operation begins with visualizing the inferior mesenteric vein (IMV). A window is created through the peritoneum under the IMV, allowing separation of Gerota's and Toldt's fascia through embryological planes. After completing gentle blunt dissection in the proper plane, the IMV is clipped and ligated safely below the pancreas.

The surgeon identifies the inferior mesenteric artery pedicle and the left colic artery for the splenic flexural cancer. The left colic artery is clipped and ligated, while the sigmoid artery is preserved to ensure adequate blood supply for the anastomosis between the transverse and sigmoid colon.

The surgeon continues dissection through Gerota's and Toldt's fascia, identifying the spleen and lateral abdominal wall. A gauze is placed on the retroperitoneum for guidance. The gastrocolic ligament is opened, and the greater omentum of the splenic flexure is ligated. Splenocolic ligaments are ligated using bipolar devices, and the lateral Toldt's fascia is opened to mobilize the left colon.

The surgeon fully mobilizes the splenic flexure, ligating the last branch of the splenocolic ligaments. The left branch of the middle colic artery is ligated near the proximal surgical margin. A laparoscopic linear stapler is used to transect the transverse colon.

The surgeon begins mobilizing the sigmoid colon, carefully identifying and preserving the left ureter, gonadal arteries, and iliac vessels. An energy device is used to thin the sigmoid colon for transection, followed by the use of a laparoscopic linear stapler to perform the transection. The specimen is externalized to the left upper quadrant. A side-to-side anastomosis is created between the transverse and sigmoid colon using the laparoscopic linear stapler, and the stapler window is closed with two-layer continuous sutures.
The specimen is extracted from the camera port using a specimen bag.
